Issue No.23 • June 	2013
Secrets Of An Entrepreneur
Who Sells $30 Million Of Wine Per Year...
JUST With EDlail
Greetings Dearest Email Player...
Last Fall I finally did my first wine tasting. My friends and family had all done it,
and had been trying to sell me on doing it for years. But it never seemed all that
interesting and fun, so I put it off. Yes, even though I live just a few hours from some of
the best wineries on the planet. And yes, even though I like a new adventure as much as
anyone. Turns out I kinda dig wine tasting. And one of the first wineries I went to had a
guy doing the pouring who made his own line of wines. We got to talking and he asked
what I did, when I said Internet marketing his eyes lit up and he practically begged me to
give him ideas on how to sell his wine online. He thought Qike many people) he needed a
FaceBook page (when thaes the LAST thing he needs), etc. He also offered to barter with
me -- giving me free wine in exchange for some consulting. But alas ... being the newly
minted wine snob I was, I turned him down. (He only sold white wines, and I don't dig on
white wine... not my bag...) In hindsight what I SHOULD have done was send him the
enclosed New York Times article about a guy who sells $30 million of wine per year
JUST using email. There's a lot of gold in this article for email marketers. And I want to
talk about the gold in them thar pages, and how you can profit from them. But first things
first, check the article out (enclosed with this issue). Then, turn to the next page...

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Interesting stuff, ain't it?
Here are some of the things that stood out while I read it:
Rimmerm.an is the founder and sole owner of Garagiste,
the world's largest e-m.ail-based wine business. With
136,000 subscribers, Rimmerm.an says that Garagiste
does, on average, $30 m.illion in annual sales offered
exclusively through his long, florid, self-m.ythologizing
daily e-m.ails. "Dear Friends, som.ewhere along the path to
wine-related enlightenment" began a recent one, which
later evoked "the incredulous 1970s chem.ical salesm.an,
dumping buckets of toxic pesticides" onto the vineyards of
poor Cham.bertin, Margaux, Latour. "At som.e point, the
land gives up. It m.ust be resuscitated over decades to fully
escape the poison (similar to sm.oking- the body
eventually cleans itself and regenerates, but a certain
scarring remains)." He then conjured lovely Sardinia,
Europe's "truest untouched terra firma," source of the
obscure 2011 Rigaterri Mirau- "Djarum. cigarette in
your glass ••• massive pine forest, clove, resin," a "once
in-a-blue-moon" steal at $18.61 a bottle.

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Note how he does DAILY emails. I harp on this month after month in this
newsletter and day after day in my own daily emails... but if you want to stick out from
the bleating herd of wannabes and jack up your sales right away, you simply gotta start
mailing daily. What I find interesting is he makes all this $$ even making amateur
mistakes like addressing his email "friends" instead of talking one-on-one.Just goes to
show, when you do it right, little things like that really don't matter all that much. And
who knows, in his case it may even help. You might also notice his emails are just flat out
INTERESTING. People wonder how you use email if you're not really solving a
problem, but instead trying to sell something people want -- and it's all about knowing
your market and researching everything there is to know about your industry. Even if you
have no interest in his wines, reading about how the land gives up and must be
resuscitated, etc is interesting in and of itsel£ At the end, if he plugs his wine (and I don't
know if he did in that one or not) you don't care that he just "pitched" you. He just took
you on a little adventure, stimulated your thinking, and, yes, entertained you. This is the
essence of email marketing. This is what practically nobody understands -- you don't have
to pitch your benefits or whatever in all your emails. You just have to be interesting, fun,
and maybe even a bit eccentric. And when you just treat your emails like they're a
conversation with someone in the market who shares your passion, a lot of the things
people find hard about writing emails disappears. Ken McCarthy brought up this point in
his "System Club Letters" book, when he said, "When you read the copy of all the
great salesmen from Claude Hopkins to David Ogilvy to Gary Bencivenga,
you'll quickly be struck by the fact that tluir ad copy doesn't sound like ad
copy. It sounds like an informed person sharing some fascinating,
worthwhile information with you."
Good stuff. And, worth keeping in mind every time you sit down to write an email,
an ad, or any other kind of sales material. Here's another part that stood out in the
article:
"Driving through Seattle's streets, he rattled otT various
nickna.Dles he claimed to have been given by unnam.ed
others: "The WIDe Whisperer. The Pied Piper of WIDe. The
Great and Powerful V~nous Oz. The (Real) Etnperor of
Wme. The Elnperor Who Has Clothes. I'll get you a list."
If someone gives you a great nickname or title -- USE IT! Milk it for all its worth
and work it into your emails. This one is probably the easiest (always been kinda hard for

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
me, though) way to help position yourself for some people. Here are some real life
examples:
• 	World's Leader In Self Defense (Captain Chris Pizzo)
• 	The Swing Surgeon (Golf PGA Master Professional and instructor Don Trahan)
• 	Doberman Dan Gallapoo (Dan Gallapoo -- the word image of a doberman
instantly sets him apart from other people)
• 	The Infomercial King (Kevin Trudeau)
• 	The Prince Of Print (The late Gary Halbert)
• 	The 42 Year Old Teenager (Raw foodist marketer Roger Haeske's title)
• 	The Doctor Of Democracy (radio talk show host Rush Limbaugh)
• 	Fure-Cat (Matt Furey)
Some people give themselves titles and names (I've been toying with that, just
haven't thought of one yet). But it's even better if someone else does, especially someone
who your market attaches importance or credibility to Qike the media, etc). Here's
another nugget from the article:
As we parked near Seattle's deepwater cargo port,
RimmerDlan said he has also been called the J. PeterDlan
of Wine, "being able to tell vivid stories in a catalog where
people buy trunks from India they have no use for." He
tried to put all this together for me: "There is an aspect of
J. Peterman. There is an aspect of Oz. There is an aspect
of the Pied Piper leading people over the edge where
they've never tasted before. But no one really knows who I
am. and how I do this. When other people try to copy what
we do, I'Dl very floored by that. It's wonderful, the
admiration."
I tell ya, this dude is my hero -- I wanna BE Jon Rimmerman when I grow up.
This is exactly the kind of air of mystery you want in your emails. You are not just selling
your product. You're taking them on interesting adventures. Showing them things they
never knew existed before. And putting a show on. People love to read emails like that,
they love to respond to emails like that and, they love to BUY from emails like that. And
like Jon Rimmerman, when you do this, you WILL be copied by the lesser mortals out
there who don't think for themselves. You WILL be aped of your style and personality
-4-

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
(although they will come off as wannabes so no biggie). And, you WILL be in awe when it
first happens. You'll probably be pissed off, too. But it's a small price to pay for success...
Now, check this out ... My FAVORITE part of the article, proving you can make
more $$ not by making it easy to find and order from you, but by (paradoxically) making
it harder to (the whole "wise man at the top of the mountain" thang... ):
Garagiste, which gets its name from a French winemaking
JDoveJDent, has not advertised since its creation in 1996.
Rimmer~nan built a Web site ouly two years ago. Before
that, you had to hear about his list through a &iend, copy
the e-mail address, then send in a polite request to join-
analogous, in soiDe ways, to the nightclub without a name,
creating desire precisely by its disinterest in attracting
you. Even today, the Garagiste Web site- through which
you can now sign up for the e-JDaillist- has no e-
comm.erce function nor even a blog post of RimmerJDan's
daily offers. You get the meiDo or you don't, and
Riuunerman rarely offers the same wine twice. Though he
blackballs people who might buy in volume to resell- the
words "no sales to retailers or wholesalers" appear in
every e- mai1 offer- he claiJDs that competitors skulk
around the list nonetheless, under fake nam.es, even
sending biJD anonymous death threats de~nanding an end
to his dangerously low prices on both inexpensive wines
(he sells plenty for under $10 a bottle) and pricey ones,
like his recent offer of 2009 Romanee Conti at $25,821 per
12-bottle case. ("Please limit requests to 1x12-pack per
person," said the acco~npanying order infor~nation, as if
that were necessary.)
Pure, unadulteratedJEENIUS -- "creating desire precisely by its disinterest in
attracting you." This is why doing "take away" selling is so powerful. It's why a guy (and
here's a tip for y'all single men ... ) will get far more female attraction by making your
interests known to a woman and then ignoring her or even "friend zoning" her
(something I enjoy doing since I started dating again after my divorce last year -- them
marketing skillz are endlessly useful ... ). And, it's why being what I call an "anti-

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
professional" - where you DON'T kiss your customers' asses ... where you DON'T whore
out your time on social media and in blog comments... and where you DON'T let just
"anyone" buy (people gotta qualify) can do more for your bottom line than all the popular
goo-roo persuasion tricks and tips combined. Also, notice the marketing spies on his list. If
you're doing email right, you'll have plenty of marketing spies-- people who are on your
list just to keep tabs on what you're doing. When this happens, you know you're doing it
right ...
RimmerDlall IDakes ordering easy enough, and also
remarkably hUDlan: every offer says something like,
"reply to this e-mail or send Nicki a note," referring to his
longtime assistant.
Of course, you SHOULD still make ordering easy. ..
He could also be "the Erin Brockovich of the wine trade,"
pointing out that pesticides have been found in bottled
wines at every price point including world famous
Bordeaux. In addition, many wineries use additives and
processing agents like egg whites, milk, fish extract,
animal gelatin (sorry, vegetarians), sugar, toasted oak
powder, the color-enhancer Mega Purple and dim.ethyl
dicarbonate, a chemical so toxic that merely inhaling it
can be fatal. Bringing attention to all this, and to little
guys doing it the old-fashioned way, Rimm.erman appeals
to the m.oral and intellectual vanity of his subscribers
before even mentioning anything as tawdry as the per-
bottle price. Only a chUDlp would buy top-dollar status-
symbol wines secredy saturated with chemicals instead of
the far-cheaper real stuff favored by true insiders.
More gold for your pocketses, Gollum -- are YOU talking about all the
shenanigans and bullshit going on in your industry? I've said it before and I'll say it again:
Calling out the fraud and nonsense in your industry is the single best way to position
yourself (and this is especially true if you're just starting in a new market). Plus, it makes
for interesting and fun email. People love drama (especially those who say they don't).
This is a way to give people their drama-fix each day while also doing good. And, of

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
course, as long as you are not engaging in the bad behavior you're calling out (and why
would you?) then you make yourself the only real choice to buy from. Also, note how he
does NOT just throw the offer at you. He builds up to it -- taking what Robert Collier
called "The creative gamble" -- a gamble your timid competitors will likely not take and,
thus, will always make less sales than they could.
And cheeky this out:
Rimm.er11l3.11's personal theory about how it all works-
how the Garagiste business model and those idiosyncratic
e-Dl8.ils compel such vigorous spending- fetishizes the
hUDlan element, the obvious im.perfections, like telling his
adm.inistrative assistant to leave typos and grammatical
errors in his e-m.ail otTers- preserving the inunediacy of
his writing- and never including photographs.
So, so cool... and we saw this same thing with Obama's emails, too, remember?
Why do these guys doing such big numbers not care about typos or grammar butchering
sentences or making things "pretty"? Because they strip away the human aspect. You
want people to know you're a human being-- warts and all--like they are. It's okay (hellz,
it's preferable!) to be "un-okay." It was one of the secrets used by Ronal Reagan ("the
great communicator") and even Columbo (the TV show).
It helps you, and rarely hurts you - and the people who don't like it and are
offended or whatever are not your customers anyway.
Finally, one last thing:
I've talked to a lot of wine aficionados over the past few months. And none of
them have ever heard of this dude's wines. You don't see them in stores, either. And, he
only ships twice per year. Yet, he does $30 million per year and has a passionate list of
over 130k people. The point is, to that list he IS a "guru." (Hellz ... he's THE guru!) He's a
leader in the market. And, he's the top of m.ind choice over all the other wines
out there - 	including the name-brand winem.akers everyone knows about,
even though he's less well known and even totally unknown in m.any cases.
And when you do email right, you get that same effect. Whether your list is 1k or I 00+ k
big, it doesn't matter.

To YOUR list, you're the guru.
They want you, not whoever the big name is in your market. This gives you a kind
of positioning almost nobody else will ever have. And can give you a secure income even
your bigger and badder competition yearns for as they go from launch to launch, working
the.JV machine and (in a frighteningly large number of cases) being just one bad launch
away from being on the street.
Anyway, there's so much gold in that article I suggest reading it several times. And
also, you can do worse than getting your bootay on his list and reading his emails and
offers:
http:// garagiste.coDl/who
Imbibe his emails, absorb them and, yes, DRINK them in.
Then, let it all influence your email writing for the rest of your days ...
50 Shades Of Great (Ernails)
One of the tips I include in the "Email Players Playbook" is to piggyback off of
popular book titles, movies and TV shows, etc. One extremely popular book is that "50
Shades Of Gray" book. And it amazed me how few marketers (at least the ones I know
about) used that title in their emails. I've done it twice so far -- and may do another one
some day, too. Especially since, both times, got some good action on the sales front.
Frankly, I think EVERYONE reading this newsletter should do a "50 shades" email. Even
if just for the fun factor (chances are you'll get some interesting feedback ...)Just substitute
"gray" with your name, your product name, or something else associated with the theme
of your email. Below are the 2 emails I did with the title. Your homework assignment (this
is mandatory, not a request!) is to do a 50 shades email. Don't copy mine, only losers do
that. Do your own spin on it. If you don't know anything about it (I've never read it,
either, I just know it's porn for chicks) just do what I did and read about it on Wikipedia
or ask someone about it who has read it. Anyway, here are the two emails, see what you
can come up with and run it ASAP.
-8-

-- 8 of 26 --

I'.
THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Subject Line: 	50 shades of gay
Once upon a time, 	my friend Jim Yaghi said in an off handed remark
(buried in an email to his list) 	that FaceBook was "gay."
And, 	he got a bunch of whiney hate mail as a result.
"What's with the gay slur Jim!"
Of course, 	he expected that.
He knew there'd be readers on his list who wouldn't grasp it was
just slang (embedded in the media, 	the culture and even the
dictionary) ••• 	and call it an attack or slur or whatever. 	(The
typical PC line -- let's face it, 	if someone is so fragile where a
mere WORD offends them, 	especially when used in a different context
than the one giving them heartburn, 	they shouldn't even be in
business to begin with).
Anyway, why bring this up?
Because Jim made lots of money from that email!
I 	think he said $5k (or more).
Like it or lump it, 	controversy sells, 	babycakes.
That's why my motto is if it ain't controversial, why bother
writing it?
You see this in the culture all the time.
For example:
Take the book "50 Shades Of Gray"
People who have read it tell me it's kind of a dorky, 	badly written
book. 	But because it's *controversial* it sells like hotcakes.
My point?
Don't be scared of controversy, my timid little bunny rabbit.
Especially in your emails.
I'm not saying to make crappy products.
-9-
www.BenSettle.com 	• 	Copyright 20 13 Ben Settle, All Rights Reserved 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC

(And hope you FAIL if you do.)
But if you do it right, 	controversy can carry the sale for you all
on it's offensive little lonesome self.
Alright enough of this clacking.

The September "Email Players" 	issue goes to print in a week.
It's a great starting issue for email newbies.

Plus, 	it's kinda controversial, 	too.

http://www.EmailPlayers.com
Ben Settle
So that's one take on it -- and yeah, it was intended to be controversial and got the
job done. Don't be afraid of controversy, embrace it. It'll give you a sleepless night or two,
but that's good for you. Because eventually you realize the only people who get super mad
are not your customers anyway. Here's another one, not controversial, but more directly
ties into my product:
Subject Line: 	50 Shades of Ben
Recently, 	I 	got the low down on "50 Shades Of Gray".
My friend Kristi said she read it, 	and how it's basically about a
guy sexually dominating and controlling some chick. 	She also said
there were parts where the dude is teasing and torturing the chick,
which only made the character want MORE such treatment.
Gotta admit, 	I was kinda turned on by that.
In an *advertising* kinda way, 	of course.
Here's what I mean:

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
It reminded me of what good bullet points in ads do.
I 	love me some good bullets.
I 	used to spend hours 	(literally -- first thing in the morning, 	on
my work breaks and lunch hour, 	and at night when I 	got home) 	each
day hand writing out bullets and sales letters by guys like Gary
Halbert, 	John Carlton, 	Scott Haines, 	Gary Bencivenga, Eugene
Schwartz and Mel Martin to get the 	"feel" for it. And nowadays some
of my best ads are just an opening paragraph and then dozens of
bullets that tease, 	and twist and taunt and, 	yes, 	*torture* the
reader so they can't sleep at night until they buy.
They just gotta scratch that ITCH.
This skill has been worth solid gold to me over the years.
Anyway, 	there's definitely an art and science to bullets.
And it doesn't come easy to most people.
Well, 	guess what?
If you'd like to learn exactly how I wrote the bullets for my
highest-earning sales letters 	(including ads that have been running
for several years without being beaten) 	I can show you.
And, 	I 	can show you for the bargain basement price of $2.99.
It's all in my new Kindle book:
"Crypto Copywriting Secrets"
This book is my exact 5-step process for writing ads.
(Bullets being 1 of the 5 steps.)
Much of the book was taken directly from a 5 issue teaching I 	did
in my old (no longer published) 	"Crypto Marketing Newsletter".
It's extremely simple to follow.
And, 	it's extremely effective, 	too.

See how that works?
You can use book titles everyone's talking about to get attention and read about
your product. You just have to think about ways to tie it in. At first it may not come easy.
But trust me, the more you do it, the easier it gets. Again, your assignment is to write your
own 50 shades email and sell your product...

Gary Bencivenga's Secret
For Mastering Copywriting
In The Fastest Tim.e Hum.anly Possible
(Works For Em.ail, Too ... )
One of the best gifts I ever received was from Ken McCarthy waaaaaay back in
2005. He had stumbled onto my website looking for a copywriter (he said I stood out from
the herd simply because I wrote so much -- which, ironically, is rare for copywriters...
something for ye freelancers to think about ...) and we hopped on the phone and started
talking. Eventually he offered to send me a copy of an interview he did with the great and
esteemed Gary Bencivenga (universally considered to be the world's greatest living
copywriter). At the time, it was the ONLY interview Gary had ever granted. And, it's not
like he's granted all that many since (maybe a small handful).
Anyway, that interview is SOLID GOLD.
I have listened to it over and over and over -- and it made me really want his
"Going away" seminar product. But one of the things that stood out the most in that
interview was Gary Bencivenga's "one a day marketing vitamin" philosophy. And that is,
where you read a control ad every day. And, even better, try to think of ways you can
improve that ad. This is no easy feat, of course (that's why it's a control ad -- nobody has
beaten it). And you know what? As awesome as this works for learning copywriting and
writing better sales letters... it's even MORE powerful in email. If you're on all the right
lists, you should be getting lots of emails each day. I certainly get my fair share (dozens per
day, at least). And every time I mentally think of ways I would do those emails better.
Maybe I'd cut out some fat. Or play with the sentences and paragraphs (shortening or
making them bigger, to make reading easier on the eye). Or use a different subject line.
Or a different call to action. Or use more slang (or LESS slang), etc etc etc.
The list goes on.
What's important is not what you'd do to make it better, but just the process of
mentally thinking this way every time you see an email (or blog post). How can it be
improved? What would you do if you HAD to ilnprove their e~nail (i.e. 1nake

m.ore sales) or you'd be beheaded? This is a great one a day marketing vitamin that
can make your emails go from good to great, and from great to epic very quickly.
And the cost?
Nothing.
It's free (although it does take time...) Hey, this may seem really simple (it IS). But if
the world's greatest living copywriter does it, maybe, just maybe ... you and I should too ...
Advice To Empower Network Members
For Writing Emails
Seems more than a few "Email Players" subscribers are members of that
Empower Network deal. And so, I get questions from time to time about how to use email
to sell their stuff. Now, before I give my opinions and thoughts on this -- a disclaimer: I
know next to nothing about Empower Network. And what I have heard does not interest
me in the slightest. I'm not saying if it's good or bad, just that it interests me about as
much an MLM company does. Plus, I think it's a mistake to sell anyone else's products on
the front end, and to instead sell your own stuff and back end other stuff if you must.
Anyway, here's a question I got a little while back, and my answer. If you're in Empower
Network, let me know if it helps. And if you're not, I think you will still find the info
useful for writing emails selling your own products, or products as an affiliate. Alright,
here's the question and my answer:
uwhat kind of emails should I write and what should I
talk about in order to promote and sell this biz-opp
of Empower Network effectively, 	using your email
marketing system, 	if I 	haven't gotten much results
yet in the biz-opp, 	and I've only made a 	little over
$2,000 dollars total online? kinda stuck there bro.
Would appreciate the help. Thankslu
OK, what you can do is first, don't worry about how much you've made (or haven't
made), etc. Think of a fat person who's on a diet, they are slowly but steadily losing
weight, but are not at their goal yet. That doesn't make her unqualified to share good
information or talk about the diet she's using that's working, even if she ain't at her

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
goal yet. I don't know much about Empower Network, so take the following and adapt,
discard and use as you see fit. But, you can do things like:
• 	Make a list of what the average person in your market is doing wrong
• 	Lists of things the pros do that are effective and work (be vague though)
• 	Tell stories about the challenges other people faced in the program and how the
reader can avoid them
• 	Talk about your own goals and then end the email with this is how I'm achieving
them day by day, etc
• 	Comment on news stories that are related to the internet or business, just give your
thoughts, tie it into checking out your link
• 	Talk about how bloggers make a lot of $$ if they know what they're doing, and
here's how a lot of them are doing it
• 	See what the leaders in your company are talking about on their blogs, then you
quote (and edify) them "so and so says ... " then give your commentary on it
• 	Research the founders of the company, talk about their lives, challenges, successes,
how they built the company, etc (make them inspirational)
• 	Talk about the problems the market is facing (this is KEY -- talk about how much
life sucks for the average person, tell real life stories of people you know or read
about, then position your product as the way to overcome-- ALWAYS be very
detailed about the problem, but vague about the solution in emails)
• 	Talk about how the political climate affects business
And the list goes on. Just ... talk -- like talk radio.
That's what I did writing 100 or so emails for a company that was somewhat
similar to Empower Network. You always want to position your product (whichever one
you're selling) as the solution to a problem or the way to achieve some cool benefit. Don't

get too hung up on your experiences or inexperiences or whatever. You don't have to talk
about yourself at all. Talk about them (the market). Their problems, their interests, their

Alright that's a wrap for this month.

October 11,2012
Drunk With Power
By DANIEL DUANE
Standing in the low-ceilinged basement of a rundown Seattle bungalow, among the shiny steel vats
and plastic tubing that constitute Animale winery, the wine merchant Jon Rimmerman swirled his
glass, sniffed its bouquet, took a sip and moved his mouth around as if chewing. A fair-skinned,
dapper and somewhat elfin man, Rimmerman wore Kelly green jeans, a lavender sweater and a
black-and-white plaid sportcoat. Salt-and-pepper curls bushed out from under his Greek
fisherman's cap as he bent over a white plastic bucket. Spitting out a great purple jet of wine,
Rimmerman signaled to the winemaker Matt Gubitosa that he could taste exactly one more vintage
before leaving.
From the outside, Animale - 	named for Gubitosa's dead but still-beloved cat, whose image has
appeared on many Animale bottles - 	looked more like a methamphetamine lab than a winery,
with an overgrown lawn, a faded gnome statue and reflective insulation covering all the basement
windows. On the inside, Animale was clean and well lighted, with all proper licensing, classic R. &
B. on the radio- "a little bit louder now!"- and a sleepy kitten.
"That's the Dolcetto?" Rimmerman asked, as Gubitosa poured.
"Two thousand ten, yes. You need some pizza with that. I use cultivated yeast, but no mechanical
anything."
Rimmerman is the founder and sole owner of Garagiste, the world's largest e-mail-based wine
business. With 136,000 subscribers, Rimmerman says that Garagiste does, on average, $30 million
in annual sales offered exclusively through his long, florid, self-mythologizing daily e-mails. "Dear
Friends, somewhere along the path to wine-related enlightenment" began a recent one, which later
evoked "the incredulous 1970s chemical salesman, dumping buckets of toxic pesticides" onto the
vineyards of poor Chambertin, Margaux, Latour. "At some point, the land gives up. It must be
resuscitated over decades to fully escape the poison (similar to smoking - 	the body eventually
cleans itself and regenerates, but a certain scarring remains)." He then conjured lovely Sardinia,
Europe's "truest untouched terra firma," source of the obscure 2011 Rigaterri Mirau- "Djarum
cigarette in your glass ... massive pine forest, clove, resin," a "once-in-a-blue-moon" steal at
$18.61 a bottle.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 26 --

Despite Animale's admirable smallness, Rimmerman was skeptical going in: only months earlier,
Robert Parker's Wine Advocate gave Gubitosa's 2009 Petit Verdot a stellar 92-point rating;
Rimmerman has built his reputation by differentiating his tastes from those of other critics,
favoring the austere, eccentric and putatively authentic over what you might call the merely
delicious. But now Rimmerman spit out another purple mouthful and said, with evident surprise,
"That's the most unusual wine." He looked Gubitosa in the eye. "I mean, not to say whether it's
good or bad, like or don't like."
Gubitosa, a burly, thin-haired so-something who works for the United States Environmental
Protection Agency when he's not cranking tunes, petting kittens and making fine wine, nodded.
Point taken.
"But it has a personality."
"Yeah, it's not for everybody," Gubitosa conceded.
"The pepper, it's incredibly crushed on the nose and through the palate, with those hard tannins,"
Rimmerman said. "There's nothing fun about that." This was a backhanded compliment. Wines of
integrity- wines of"character and terroir," to use Rimmerman's term- aim not to please but to
express what Rimmerman calls, in all seriousness, "vinous truth," meaning the honest expression
of a particular grape varietal, grown in a particular place, in a particular year. "People have distilled
my life down to, He's in pursuit of the truth, more broadly, in all things," Rimmerman says.
Rimmerman shook Gubitosa's hand, explaining that he had a flight booked for later that evening,
over to the wine country of southeastern Washington, for a few more days of tasting. From there,
Rimmerman had a flight booked to Washington, D.C., where his first East Coast warehouse is
under construction. Rimmerman says he has spent about half of the last 15 years on the road,
hunting wine and story in Europe, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, South America, Turkey,
Israel, three Canadian provinces, northern Mexico and 13 American states.
Stepping into the Seattle downpour, Rimmerman trotted delicately across the sad lawn, climbed
into the car and insisted that his personal preference - 	the question of whether or not he would
drink Gubitosa's wines in his own home - 	was irrelevant.
"What I'm trying to uncover is something that is culturally important or of the moment, which this
definitely is," Rimmerman said. "This is cutting-edge Washington State winemaking. So, first: Are
the wines sound? And then, would people that read everything that I write, every day" - 	in those
Garagiste e-mail offers - 	"be interested not just for the wine but for the story, the cats, the meth
lab, the geologist, the maybe-no-woman-in-his-life. Would they like to kind of taste that story in
the bottle." The answer was maybe: Gubitosa's wines were not "immediately crowd pleasing, but he
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 26 --

was trying to make something real, that was not doctored." In Rimmerman's cosmology of the wine
• industry, which holds that a vast majority of wine on the U.S. market goes through industrial
processing aimed at pleasing Robert Parker, this was high praise indeed.
Driving through Seattle's streets, he rattled off various nicknames he claimed to have been given by
unnamed others: "The Wine Whisperer. The Pied Piper of Wine. The Great and Powerful Vinous
Oz. The (Real) Emperor of Wine. The Emperor Who Has Clothes. I'll get you a list."
"The Wine Whisperer" refers to Rimmerman's conviction that he, along with his partner and their
6-year-old daughter, all have superhuman tasting powers that may or may not qualify them as
what are called "supertasters," a distinction thought to have a legitimate anatomical basis. In
addition, Rimmerman will say that his daughter has a genetically enhanced tactile sense - 	that she
is a kind of superfeeler - 	and that he, personally, by simply looking at a label, can recall the taste
of any wine that has ever crossed his lips, a number running into the many tens of thousands. This
is an aptitude that, he feels quite sure, even Parker cannot claim. "This is something that people
have called a photographic memory of wine," Rimmerman told me. "Or they're not quite sure what
it is. It hasn't been categorized yet."
Those two "Emperor" nicknames, playing off the Robert Parker biography "The Emperor of Wine,"
evoke the doubtless pleasant fantasy that Rimmerman - 	despite being a salesman with no medium
beyond his own marketing copy- might somehow displace Parker as the great American wine
tastemaker. "I don't think of myself as a retailer or an importer," Rimmerman likes to say. "I think
of myself as a writer and a conduit of culture."
As we parked near Seattle's deepwater cargo port, Rimmerman said he has also been called the J.
Peterman of Wine, ''being able to tell vivid stories in a catalog where people buy trunks from India
they have no use for." He tried to put all this together for me: "There is an aspect of J. Peterman.
There is an aspect of Oz. There is an aspect of the Pied Piper leading people over the edge where
they've never tasted before. But no one really knows who I am and how I do this. When other
people try to copy what we do, I'm very floored by that. It's wonderful, the admiration."
Stepping into the rain, just then, he opened an unmarked door in a concrete wall and led me into
his cavernous warehouse filled to the ceiling with countless cases of wine. His partner, Shira
Young, stood nearby, a striking woman with jet-black hair, dark eyes, golden skin and a soothing
and sensible demeanor that reflects well on the manic Rimmerman. She and one of their
daughters, Gigi, hovered near a tall round table covered with olive oil both in bottles and on plates.
Rimmerman asked Gigi if she had tasted them.
Shira replied for her. "She tasted the entire table."
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 26 --

Gigi did not look up; she was busy drawing her little hands through a plate of olive oil and then
dreamily rubbing her fingers together.
Garagiste, which gets its name from a French winemaking movement, has not advertised since
its creation in 1996. Rimmerman built a Web site only two years ago. Before that, you had to hear
about his list through a friend, copy the e-mail address, then send in a polite request to join -
analogous, in some ways, to the nightclub without a name, creating desire precisely by its
disinterest in attracting you. Even today, the Garagiste Web site- through which you can now sign
up for the e-mail list - 	has no e-commerce function nor even a blog post of Rimmerman's daily
offers. You get the memo or you don't, and Rimmerman rarely offers the same wine twice. Though
he blackballs people who might buy in volume to resell- the words "no sales to retailers or
wholesalers" appear in every e-mail offer - 	he claims that competitors skulk around the list
nonetheless, under fake names, even sending him anonymous death threats demanding an end to
his dangerously low prices on both inexpensive wines (he sells plenty for under $10 a bottle) and
pricey ones, like his recent offer of 2009 Romanee Conti at $25,821 per 12-bottle case. ("Please
limit requests to 1x12-pack per person," said the accompanying order information, as if that were
necessary.)
Rimmerman makes ordering easy enough, and also remarkably human: every offer says something
like, "reply to this e-mail or send Nicki a note," referring to his longtime assistant. But he also
requires a tolerance for deferred gratification, shipping only twice a year, once in the spring and
once in the fall, when Rimmerman says extreme heat or cold are less likely to destroy the wine in
transit. In his wine offerings, he told me in a personal e-mail, he strives to reach his entire
demographic simultaneously, "from Brooklyn 'coolio' (Williamsburg, Red Hook) to waning coolio
(Park Slope) to Upper West Side to Scarsdale," as he puts it, "with vastly different levels of
disposable income." Thus, obscure cheap wines for "the lowest-disposable-income group with the
most cutting-edge knowledge of the wine world and the most time to spend on their computer (23-
to-33-year-olds in Brooklyn, the Mission, Echo Park in L.A., etc.)" are offered in the same e-mail as
wildly expensive ones, the whole thing stitched together by, say, "Beach Boys songs and bizarre,
little-known facts or idiosyncrasies of Brian Wilson."
Rimmerman was born in 1966 and raised on Chicago's North Shore until his parents divorced and
Rimmerman's mother and stepfather took him to live in rural Wisconsin. He remained close to a
father he describes, with characteristic brio, as a kind of supersalesman - 	claiming he invented
commodities-trading algorithms "before computers," created "one of the first pro-athlete
management agencies" only to sell it before it became profitable, and helped the N.F .L. develop the
whole idea of staging exhibition games overseas.
"He made millions and he lost millions and he was never afraid to go for it," Rimmerman told me.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 26 --

"That's definitely my personality and the impetus for Garagiste- no fear, believing in yourself."
Garagiste itself emerged from an ad hoc wine-tasting club at the University of Wisconsin-Madison.
After finals one year, Rimmerman and a few classmates celebrated by pooling money to buy a 1979
Krug Champagne and 1982 Dom Perignon. "It was one of those 'Oh, my God' moments, there's
something of a higher order here," he said. "I sat with those wines for 15 hours."
It soon emerged among the group that Rimmerman had the most sensitive palate and a gift for
spinning great stories about interesting wines, and it wasn't long, he says, before he found himself
faxing an informal wine letter to friends scattered around the country.
During Rimmerman's years at DePaul University law school, he landed a summer job in Chicago,
at the first American Starbucks outside Seattle. While jerking espresso and sweeping the floors
there, he happened to meet the company's founder, Howard Schultz.
"So I was part of the whole genesis of, whoa, what Starbucks was," Rimmerman says. "I'll never
forget the incredible passion he had for a very simple idea that he was absolutely 100 percent
certain would be important to the culture of the United States in the next 20 years." Rimmerman
says he learned from his time at Starbucks "the beauty of retail marketing, of conceptual ideology
with consumer goods," an idea that can be roughly translated as defining a crystal-clear brand
identity and then ensuring that everything from the product to customer relations reinforces it.
Rimmerman was living in Seattle when his own simple idea fell into place. "That was right around
the same time as the whole Seattle, Nirvana, Internet, you know, boom, of incredible creative
energy," he says, aligning his own origin story with those. "I met all these incredible, smart people
inventing all these things called, like, Adobe. I mean, Bezos was running Amazon out of a garage.
And everybody knew them!"
He realized he had the seeds of his own start-up in that wine letter. He'd already switched toe-
mail, picking up several hundred new subscribers who often asked where to buy the wines. It was a
no-brainer to start selling them. All he needed now, Rimmerman decided, was a clear sense of
mission - 	a brand identity, in other words, centered on making the world a better place instead of
just making a buck. Rimmerman claims that he made two lists - 	things he liked about the wine
business, and things he did not - 	and decided that his company's goal would be to transform the
industry until his "don't like" column was empty.
He's a little vague about what was actually on those lists - 	you suspect that he has, to be generous,
lost the originals. But he is consistent in characterizing his primary don't-like: an intolerable gulf
between winemakers and wine drinkers caused by a legally mandated separation of importers,
distributors and retailers. "It's one of the only systems in our country- women's right to vote, all
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 26 --

these things that have changed over time, the whole racial thing that happened in the '6os - 	that
the government never wanted to look at," Rimmerman says.
By cutting out all those middlemen, Rimmerman could offer lower prices at higher profit margins,
while becoming the "Sub Pop Records of the wine trade," scouting talent and connecting old-school
vintners to discriminating consumers. He could also be "the Erin Brockovich of the wine trade,"
pointing out that pesticides have been found in bottled wines at every price point including world
famous Bordeaux. In addition, many wineries use additives and processing agents like egg whites,
milk, fish extract, animal gelatin (sorry, vegetarians), sugar, toasted oak powder, the color-
enhancer Mega Purple and dimethyl dicarbonate, a chemical so toxic that merely inhaling it can be
fatal. Bringing attention to all this, and to little guys doing it the old-fashioned way, Rimmerman
appeals to the moral and intellectual vanity of his subscribers before even mentioning anything as
tawdry as the per-bottle price. Only a chump would buy top-dollar status-symbol wines secretly
saturated with chemicals instead of the far-cheaper real stuff favored by true insiders.
Rimmerman is neither the first nor the only wine merchant to have built a brand identity around
the distinction between industrial and artisanal winemaking. Kermit Lynch, for example, a minor
legend in the international wine trade from Berkeley, Calif., who says he has never heard of
Rimmerman, has been importing French wines of this stripe since the 1970s. Then there is
Chambers Street Wines, a brick-and-mortar retail shop in Manhattan that makes a comparable
commitment to selling noninterventionist wines.
But Rimmerman has discovered some of the great noninterventionist standard-bearers, like Frank
Cornelissen, who was toiling in obscurity high above the snow line on Sicily's volcanic Mount Etna,
before Rimmerman found him resurrecting abandoned vineyards and vinifying the juice in the
strictest of Old World methods. According to Alder Yarrow, a Garagiste fan and author of the
influential wine blog Vinography: "Cornelissen just takes a bunch of grapes, throws them in
buckets, stomps them, comes back six months later and puts it in bottles. They are the most
natural wines in the world." The result is cloudy with visible sediment, and even prone to
refermenting in the bottle. "But when they are good, they are unbelievable!" Yarrow says.
"Rimmerman likes that kind of thing, wines that to most Americans are like eeuwee!"
Of all the purported nicknames Rimmerman offers, the most telling may be "the Great and
Powerful Vinous Oz" - 	celebrating, as it does, a certain Emerald City quality in Garagiste.
Rimmerman has doubtless traveled around the world seeking great wine, but he also appears to
find at least some through traditional distributors and importers - 	one of whom, asking to remain
anonymous because he does business with Rimmerman, explained that Garagiste can be a
convenient way to move a lot of inventory in a big hurry.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 26 --

Alice Feiring, a New York-based wine writer, claims that Rimmerman has even discovered wines
.. through her blog posts. (Rimmerman, who considers Feiring a fellow traveler, insists the timing of
their discovery was merely coincidental.) "He's not a tastemaker," she says. "He is picking up on a
trend. He is a businessman." Feiring adds, however, that she knows "people who are very, very,
very faithful to him, and give him a lot of money all the time." Other industry insiders have told me
similar things, raising one of the greater curiosities of the Garagiste phenomenon: Rimmerman's
act seems to appeal most powerfully to people with no illusion about how it works. David
Schildknecht, for example - 	one of the most prominent wine writers in the world and a critic for
The Wine Advocate, and therefore a man deluged with free wine samples - 	chuckled over the
phone, saying: "I buy wine from him regularly..... It's pretty amusing to me." Michael Terrien, a
boutique Napa winemaker, calls Rimmerman's daily e-mails "wine crack," adding that he has to
unsubscribe periodically to stop the financial hemorrhage.
Rimmerman's personal theory about how it all works -how the Garagiste business model and
those idiosyncratic e-mails compel such vigorous spending - 	fetishizes the human element, the
obvious imperfections, like telling his administrative assistant to leave typos and grammatical
errors in his e-mail offers- preserving the immediacy of his writing- and never including
photographs. "Psychologically, it's very important," he says. "If I told you that story but you didn't
like the look of the label" - 	Animale's cat, say- you might doubt the pitch. ("This is something
I've carried for 18 years," Rimmerman told me, as if confessing a terrible secret. "I've never told
anybody this.") He also cites a broader cultural shift working in his favor- "It's almost like
'everything old is new again,' "he wrote in a personal e-mail to me. "Or the music scene going back
to turntables or ... vintage 1960s tube amplifiers - 	people crave warmth, whether its auditory or
in business, and eventually they come around to what makes them feel good, what keeps them
warm - 	sensory or mentally."
This idea of analog musical warmth is central to his thinking. In Walla Walla, Wash., after a long
morning among the giant grain silos, vast wheat fields, not-so-vast vineyards, and smelly horse
corrals of the area's rural fringes, we stopped at the relatively elegant Waters Winery. Jamie
Brown, a vintner who made a living selling bootleg concert CDs during the Seattle grunge scene of
the 1990s, is now a sort ofFalstaffian rocker/poet/artiste who has sold many wines through
Rimmerman. Inside the barrel-aging chamber, Rimmerman tasted multiple vintages of Waters
syrah and cabernet, free-associating toward a sales pitch: "This is cabernet sauvignon for cabernet
sauvignon's sake ... not for oak's influence, Napa Valley, prestige, Robert Parker." He spat into a
floor drain and took a second stab: "This is the Old World that has come to America. This is like
Ellis Island in Washington State. How about that for a quote? That's a big thing for me to say,
because I've never found that anywhere."
Four months later, offering a Waters syrah called Tremolo, Rimmerman instead wrote about
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 26 --

Monteverdi inventing musical tremolo about 400 years ago. "He never could have imagined ...
Liszt would use it as a palpitating piano technique or Bo Diddley on the guitar. From Floyd Rose
reinventing the double-locking wheel, to the classic blackface circuitry of early 1960s Fender Twin
Reverb amps (or VoxAC30, Silvertone, etc.)." He followed this arguably absurd but possibly
masterful massaging of his audience with a reassurance to all that Tremolo had an impeccable Old
World anti-mainstream taste- "mineral salts ... natural (ripe) acidity, 12.5% alcohol." Then he
took a quick detour into his global travels - 	"scurrying about on back roads in Turkey, Croatia,
Ribeiro, Sicily" - 	and made a brief pause to make sure we all knew he really had been in Walia
Walla, personally, shaking that winemaker's hand. ''When I first sampled it a few months ago with
Jamie," Rimmerman wrote of the wine, "I was so taken with its truth that I asked to put the offer
out then and there (on my BlackBerry, in typical Garagiste fashion)."
I have no recollection of this exchange, nor of any whispered conversation between Rimmerman
and Brown beyond my earshot. But Rimmerman swears it happened and, at $39.99 a bottle, it
almost certainly doesn't matter.
Daniel Duane is the author of "How to Cook Like a Man: A Memoir of Cookbook Obsession."

do something I've used for the last few years to create some extremely profitable product launches with
using pretty much just email.
Something easy, simple and ...
Want to know what it is?
Well, I'll give you a hint:
That Gets Exnonentially
More Sales Than Normal.
If you've ever watchedAMC throughout the day when they launch a new season of"The
Walking Dead", you probably noticed a count down timer on the screen moving towards the time when
the new episode airs.
It counts down ALL day.
And it's there constantly during all their shows.
Plus, they continually plug the premiere episode in commercials throughout the day, too, so you
can't possibly NOT know about it.

Anyway, so there's the hint.
It's a powerful concept that's one part of my launch process (using mostly just email-- no JV's,
social media, blogs, videos or anything time consuming required) that has worked like gangbusters.
And before you ask ... it not only has worked for me, but clients, too.
In fact, we KILLED it with this method in the golf and weight loss niches.
And I continue to kill it whenever I use it (as have others I've shown it to).
Anyway, I taught my entire launch methodology in the December 2011 "Email Players" issue.
